originalTitle: 'The gravest crime against humanity': What does the UN vote on slavery mean?
title: A Big Talk About Fairness for Africa!
content:
Hello, my amazing friends across Africa! I'm your friendly news reporter, ready to share a very important story with you today. It's a story about fairness, history, and making things right. It might sound a little grown-up, but I promise to explain it simply, just for you! This story is especially important for our beloved continent, Africa.

A Sad Story from Long Ago: What Was Slavery?

Once upon a time, many, many years ago, something very sad and very wrong happened. It was called slavery. Slavery meant that some people were taken from their homes, especially from Africa. They were forced to work for other people. They didn't get to choose. They didn't get paid. They couldn't be free. This was very, very unfair and it caused a lot of pain. Imagine someone telling you what to do all day, every day. You couldn't go to school. You couldn't play with your friends. You couldn't even live where you wanted. That's a bit like what slavery was, but much, much worse. People were treated like objects, not like wonderful human beings. This terrible time lasted for a very, very long time. It was a dark chapter in history. Many people from Africa were taken far away across the big ocean to different lands. **Did you know?** Slavery happened for hundreds of years, like a very, very long time ago, even before your grandparents' grandparents were born!

Who Are African and Caribbean Nations?

Now, let's talk about "nations." A nation is just another word for a country. So, when we say "African nations," we mean countries right here on our beautiful continent of Africa. Countries like Nigeria, Kenya, South Africa, Ghana, and many, many more! The "Caribbean nations" are countries that are on many small islands far away across the ocean. Many, many people who live in these Caribbean countries today are descendants of people who were taken from Africa during slavery. They share a very special link with us! These countries, both in Africa and the Caribbean, are like a big family. They remember the sad past. They want to make sure that such a bad thing never happens again. They also want to talk about how the past still affects them today, in their towns and villages. **Did you know?** Many people in Caribbean countries have family roots that trace back to different parts of Africa!

What Does "Compensation" Mean?

This is an important word: "compensation." It means making things right after something bad has happened. Imagine your friend accidentally breaks your favourite toy. What would be fair? Maybe they help you fix it, or they help you get a new one. That's a kind of compensation. In our story, "compensation" means that the countries that got rich and powerful because of slavery should help the countries that suffered. They should help to build schools, hospitals, and good roads. They should help make life better for the people whose ancestors suffered so much. It's not just about money. It's about saying a real "sorry" for the past. It's about understanding that the past still makes some things hard for people today. It's about helping to create a brighter future for everyone, especially for young people like you.

Who Should Help to Make Things Right?

During the time of slavery, some countries in Europe became very, very wealthy. They got rich by using the free labour of enslaved people. People were forced to work on big farms growing things like sugar, cotton, and tobacco. These things were then sold for a lot of money. So, the African and Caribbean nations are saying, "Those countries that benefited from this terrible past should now help us." They want the rich countries to help build better lives in the places that were hurt by slavery. It's about sharing some of that old wealth to mend old wounds. It's like if someone ate all your snacks and then had a lot of energy, while you were still hungry. It would be fair if they shared their next meal with you, right? It's about balance and fairness, even for countries. **Did you know?** The sugar that people put in their tea and coffee, and the cotton for many clothes, were often grown by enslaved people in the past!

Why Is This Talk Happening Now? What is the UN?

Why are we talking about something that happened so long ago, today? Because the effects of slavery are still felt. Some countries are still struggling because of this very bad past. They want to heal and grow stronger. They want to give their children the best chance in life. And where are these important talks happening? At a very special place called the UN. UN stands for United Nations. Imagine a very, very big club where almost all the countries in the world are members. That's the UN! The UN is a place where leaders from all over the world come together. They talk about big problems. They try to find peaceful solutions. They try to make the world a safer and fairer place for everyone. It's like a big meeting place for the whole world's family! **Did you know?** Almost 200 countries are part of the United Nations! It's truly a global family!

What Did the Countries Vote For at the UN?

Recently, something important happened at the UN. The countries had a big discussion. They decided to keep talking about slavery and its impact. They voted to explore how to make things right. This is called a "vote." It means that many countries agreed that this is an important topic. They agreed that countries should talk about compensation. They agreed that countries should work together to heal the wounds of the past. It's like saying, "Yes, this is a real problem, and we need to work on it!" This vote is a first step, a very important first step. It shows that more and more countries are listening. They understand that the past has a big shadow. They want to make sure that everyone can have a bright future.

Is It Easy to Make Things Right?

Well, my friends, it's not always easy. Just like sometimes it's hard for all your friends to agree on a game to play, it's hard for many countries to agree on big things. There are many different ideas and opinions. Some countries who were involved in slavery in the past might not want to pay compensation. They might say it happened too long ago. But the African and Caribbean nations believe it's very important to address it. They believe it's about fairness and justice for everyone. So, it's like a big puzzle that needs many pieces to fit together. It will take time. It will take lots of talking and understanding from everyone. But talking is always a good start! **Did you know?** Talking and sharing ideas are super important ways to solve big problems, even for grown-ups and whole countries!

Why Is Slavery Called "The Gravest Crime Against Humanity"?

The word "gravest" means the most serious, the worst. "Crime against humanity" means something that hurts people in a way that is so bad, it hurts all of humanity. Slavery was truly one of the worst things that ever happened. It was a terrible, terrible wrong. It hurt millions of people. It took away their freedom, their families, and their dignity. It caused pain that lasted for generations, meaning it even affected children and grandchildren. That's why it's called "the gravest crime against humanity." It was a terrible mistake that should never, ever be repeated. Remembering this helps us learn from history. It helps us promise to always treat everyone with kindness, respect, and fairness. Every person deserves to be free and happy.

What Happens Next?

Now that the UN has voted, the talking will continue. Countries will meet again. They will share their ideas. They will try to find good ways to make things fairer for the future. They will explore how to help the countries that suffered. This is a long journey, but it's an important one. It's a journey towards more fairness, more understanding, and more peace in our world. It's a journey to heal old hurts and build a stronger, happier future for Africa and all its wonderful people. Thank you for listening to this important story, my friends! Keep learning, keep asking questions, and always stand up for fairness and kindness!
summary: African and Caribbean countries are asking for help (compensation) from countries that became rich from the very sad history of slavery, and the world's big club, the UN, has voted to keep talking about how to make things fair.
whyMatters: This matters because it's about making things fair for countries that suffered a long time ago, helping them grow stronger now, and learning from history so everyone can have a kind and happy future.

originalTitle: Women secretly filmed, then ridiculed and abused online
title: Always Ask First: Being Safe with Pictures and Videos!
content:
Hello, my amazing friends all over Africa! I'm your friendly news reporter, and I'm here to tell you super important things happening around us. News helps us learn and grow! Today, we're going to talk about something really important: being safe and kind when we use cameras and the internet. It's like learning how to cross the road safely, but for pictures and videos!

What Happened? A Secret Video Story

Imagine you're playing with your friends. Someone takes a picture or a video of you, but they don't ask you first! How would that make you feel? Maybe a little surprised? A little shy? Well, something like this happened to some grown-up ladies in two wonderful countries in Africa: Kenya and Ghana. These countries are full of sunshine and amazing people! A man came up to some ladies and filmed them. But here's the tricky part: he did not ask them if it was okay to film them. He just filmed them secretly. That means he took videos without asking for their permission. *Did you know?* Kenya is famous for its amazing wildlife, like lions and elephants! Ghana is known for its delicious jollof rice and beautiful kente cloth!

Why Asking is Super Important!

When someone takes your picture or video without asking, it's like someone taking your favourite toy without asking you first. It doesn't feel very nice, does it? Asking for permission is like saying, "Please, may I?" It's a kind and respectful thing to do. If someone wants to take your picture, they should always, always ask you first. And you have the right to say "yes" or "no"! It's your face, your picture, your choice! This man took videos of these ladies without asking. That was not okay. Everyone has a right to their own space and to decide if they want to be on camera or not. This is called 'privacy'. *Did you know?* In many parts of Africa, people greet each other with warm smiles and often ask "How are you?" as a sign of respect and care!

What is Privacy?

Let's talk about 'privacy'. It's a big word, but it means something simple. Your privacy is like your special secret garden. It's things about you that you want to keep just for yourself, or share only with people you trust very much. Your drawings are private until you want to show them. Your feelings are private until you want to talk about them. Your face and your actions are private too. Nobody should record them and show them to the world without your permission. It's about respecting you! When someone films you without asking, they are not respecting your privacy. They are going into your "secret garden" without an invitation.

The Internet and What Happened Next

After the man filmed the ladies secretly, he did something else that was not kind. He put the videos on the internet for everyone to see. The internet is like a giant, super-fast post office where people can share pictures, videos, and messages with people all over the world. It can be a fun place to learn and connect! But sometimes, people use the internet in not-so-kind ways. When these videos of the ladies were put online, other people started saying mean things about them. They made fun of them. This is called 'ridiculing' or 'abusing' someone online. Imagine someone laughing at your drawing or saying mean things about your clothes. It makes you feel sad, right? These ladies felt sad and worried because people were being mean to them on the internet. *Did you know?* The internet can help us learn about amazing animals, watch fun cartoons, and even talk to family far away! But we need to use it smartly.

Being Brave and Speaking Up

These ladies were very brave! They told grown-ups about what happened. They told a big news company called the BBC. The BBC helps share important stories so everyone can learn. It's super important to be brave and tell a grown-up you trust if something like this ever happens to you, or to someone you know. A grown-up could be your mom, dad, grandma, grandpa, teacher, or any trusted adult. They will help you! Don't ever keep a secret that makes you feel sad, scared, or worried. Good secrets are like surprise birthday parties, but bad secrets are like heavy rocks that make your heart feel heavy. *Did you know?* Your voice is powerful! When you speak up about something wrong, you help make things better for everyone.

How Can We Be Safe Online?

The internet is a wonderful place, but like crossing the street, we need to know the rules to stay safe. 1. **Always Ask First:** If you want to take a picture or video of someone, always, always ask them if it's okay. And listen to their answer! 2. **Say "No":** If someone tries to film you and you don't want them to, it's okay to say "No!" loudly and clearly. Then, tell a grown-up right away. 3. **Think Before You Share:** Before you put anything online – a picture, a video, a message – ask yourself: Is this kind? Is this safe? Would my grown-up be happy if they saw this? 4. **Tell a Grown-Up:** If you ever see anything online that makes you feel uncomfortable, scared, or sad, or if you see someone being mean, tell a trusted grown-up immediately. They can help you! 5. **Be a Friend:** If you see someone being filmed without permission, or being made fun of online, be a kind friend and tell a grown-up. You can help protect your friends too! *Did you know?* Your school or grown-ups might have special rules for using phones and cameras to keep everyone safe and happy!

Being Kind is the Best!

We learned today that it's super important to be kind and respectful to everyone, online and offline. We should always ask for permission before taking pictures or videos. And we should never, ever say mean things about people, especially not on the internet. Let's all promise to be kind internet users! Let's use the internet to learn, to share happy things, and to connect with friends and family in a good way. Remember, every person in Africa and all over the world deserves to be treated with kindness and respect. We are all special and unique! *Did you know?* Sharing kind words and helping others can make your own heart feel happy too!

A Message for All Our Young Heroes

You are smart, you are brave, and you are powerful! You can make a big difference by always asking for permission, respecting other people's privacy, and telling a grown-up if something doesn't feel right. Let's keep our African communities, and our online spaces, full of respect, kindness, and joy! Thank you for listening, my wonderful friends. See you next time with more exciting news!
summary: Some ladies in Kenya and Ghana were filmed without asking and then made fun of online, teaching us to always ask permission and be kind and safe on the internet.
whyMatters: This matters because everyone deserves respect and privacy, and we all need to learn how to be safe and kind when we use cameras and the internet.

originalTitle: South Africa's exclusion from G7 summit no surprise, says Ramaphosa
title: Our President, Mr. Ramaphosa, and a Big Countries Meeting!
content:
Hello, little news explorers! Welcome to your special news show from Africa! Today, we have a very interesting story about our country, South Africa, and our wonderful President.

Meet Our President!

Our President is Mr. Cyril Ramaphosa. He is the leader of our big and beautiful country, South Africa. Just like your mom or dad leads your family, the President leads our whole country. He works very hard to make sure everyone in South Africa is safe and happy. Did you know? South Africa is at the very bottom of Africa! It has amazing animals like lions and elephants, and lovely beaches too!

What is a Country?

A country is a special place where many people live together. It has its own rules and its own leader. Our country is South Africa. Other countries are like our neighbours in the big world.

What is the G7?

Imagine you have a club with your best friends. The G7 is like a very important club for seven big and powerful countries in the world. These countries are Canada, France, Germany, Italy, Japan, the United Kingdom, and the United States. They are called the 'Group of Seven' or G7 for short. They often meet to talk about big problems and how to help each other and other countries. Did you know? The G7 countries represent a big part of the world's money and businesses! They have lots of important discussions.

What is a Summit?

A summit is a very important meeting. It's like a big playdate, but for grown-up leaders from different countries! When leaders go to a summit, they sit together and talk about important things. They discuss how to make the world a better place, like making sure everyone has food or keeping our planet clean. This special summit we are talking about was going to be held in a beautiful country called France. France is in Europe, very far from us.

An Invitation from France

France was hosting this big G7 meeting. The President of France wanted to invite other important leaders too, not just the G7 club members. He wanted to hear ideas from leaders all over the world. So, France sent a special invitation to our President, Mr. Ramaphosa, from South Africa. It was an invitation to come to the big meeting! It's like getting an invitation to a big birthday party! South Africa is a very important country in Africa. Our President was happy to get the invitation. He wanted to share our country's ideas and listen to others.

A Big Problem!

But then, something tricky happened. There was another very big country called the United States of America. At that time, its leader was a man named Mr. Trump. Reports say that Mr. Trump did not want Mr. Ramaphosa to come to the meeting. He asked France very strongly to take back the invitation. This is what we call 'pressure'. Imagine if you invited a friend to your party, but then another friend said, "If they come, I won't!" That's a bit like what happened.

The Invitation Was Taken Back

Because of this strong request from Mr. Trump, France decided to take back the invitation to our President. This means Mr. Ramaphosa was told he could not come to the G7 meeting after all. Taking back an invitation can make people feel a little sad or confused. It means someone was invited, but then they were un-invited.

Our President's Feelings

Our President, Mr. Ramaphosa, was not surprised by this news. He said, "It's no surprise." This means he might have thought something like this could happen. It's like when you expect something tricky to happen, and then it does. He understood that big countries sometimes have different ideas and sometimes those ideas can cause changes like this.

Why is it Important for Countries to Talk?

Even if things get a bit tricky sometimes, it is very important for countries to talk to each other. When leaders meet, they can become friends. They can help each other with problems. Talking helps countries work together to make sure people have food, good schools, and safe homes. It helps to keep the peace in the world. Did you know? Leaders from different countries often shake hands and smile, even if they don't agree on everything. It's a way of showing respect and friendship!

South Africa is Still Strong!

Even though our President did not go to this particular G7 meeting, South Africa is still a very important and strong country. Mr. Ramaphosa continues to work hard for all of us. We have so many amazing things in South Africa, from our beautiful mountains to our diverse cultures. Our country has its own special voice in the world, and it will always be heard. Did you know? South Africa has 11 official languages! That means we have many ways to say "hello"! Like "Sawubona" in Zulu or "Molo" in Xhosa! So, my little news friends, remember that leaders try their best for their countries. Sometimes things are tricky, but being kind, talking, and trying to understand each other are always the best ways forward. We are proud of South Africa and our President!
